chapters.title: Welcome to the kingdom of trees
chapters.text:
Trees aren’t seen as the most intelligent creatures; they don’t come off as social beings either. But studies have shown that trees talk to each other through a series of roots and a fungal network around the root tips that facilitate nutrient exchange. Trees use this to support each other; they form a lasting and symbiotic relationship solely responsible for the longevity that trees enjoy.

Trees can communicate with one another. They don’t use sounds or language, but with scents and electrical pulses through the root network they have established.

About 20 years ago, Peter Wohlleben began to organize survival training and log-cabin tours for tourists. Then he built an old forest preserve and a location in the forest where individuals might be buried as an alternative to regular graveyards. Visitors were enchanted by crooked, gnarled trees that Wohlleben had previously written off because of their low commercial value.

Walking with visitors, Wohlleben learned to pay attention to more than just the quality of the trees’ trunks. He began to notice bizarre root shapes, peculiar growth patterns, and mossy cushions on the bark. His love of nature was thus reignited. Suddenly, Wohlleben experienced an unexplainable epiphany with trees. Coincidentally, Aachen University (RWTH Aachen) began conducting regular scientific research programs in the forest he manages. Many questions were answered throughout this investigation, but many more arose.

Wohlleben's life as a forester was once again thrilling. Every day in the forest was a new adventure for him, and this led to some unorthodox forest management techniques. When you know that trees feel pain and have memories, and that tree parents live alongside their children, you can no longer just cut them down and interrupt their lives with enormous machinery.

By going through this summary, you are invited to share the joy trees can bring to humanity. And, who knows, perhaps on your next walk in the forest, you will discover for yourself wonders great and small.

chapters.title: Trees have a grand, colorful, and rich behavioral syntax that orders their growth and longevity
chapters.text:
Communication between trees is essential to warn the neighboring trees about dangers like insects and animals that wish to eat their leaves or bark. Unfortunately, the trees that are prey to insects and herbivores are isolated from the root network and don’t get a prior warning from the nearby trees.

When trees struggle due to a lack of sunlight or other factors that cause the tree to weaken or wither, they have a sturdy support system that keeps them growing, albeit slowly. The trees naturally develop deep roots that stretch and connect to other trees.

The root network of a cluster of trees sustains the group’s struggling members; thus, even when trees near their expiration, they can live longer than that.

As the lives of trees hold such significant complexities, there are still more things to discover in the habits and quirks. For example, trees also share in the ritual of love and reproduction. With the aid of external agents such as wind and insects that spread and disperse seedlings of the trees to other fertile soil, to keep propagating the existence of the tree species.

Trees live long, really long; the average tree lives 115 times longer than a human, around 9000 years. And their growth is slow, prolonged, and this often is due to the parent trees doting over the new offspring. So when a young tree is growing, it could, of course, grow 18 inches per day, but they are always in the shadows of larger neighboring trees that starve it of sunshine, more giant, and as such, it will be unable to produce enough chlorophyll needed to grow as quickly as it should.

There is an unspoken rule among the trees as they grow and exist in the forest together. However, there is a variation of growth patterns due to the tree’s position, the exposure to light, and the number of trees within the vicinity. Trees grow in favor of the weather, the wind strength, and the availability of sunlight. Often, these trees have to grow away or toward the extending branches of competing trees.

The silent rules that govern the forest make it easier for them to coexist without any harmful interference from the neighboring trees.

chapters.title: Trees are incredibly resilient organisms that show an astounding capacity to thrive and sustain their existence through adaptive learning
chapters.text:
Another wonder the world of trees holds was previously reserved for sentient beings or at least intelligent animals. Trees can take in information, learn and use the information to adapt themselves to their environment and whatever changes occur. For example, when a tree is faced with great strain due to the weight of its crown, it thickens the bark around the area where the strain is felt to toughen up the resistance.

Trees can learn, even though their sensory devices are limited to a few primary/primitive techniques.

They are also capable of great partnerships; trees are known to team up with different species to bolster their overall performance. For example, the symbiotic relationship between trees and a specific mycelium species of a fungus that suits the tree’s needs works out fine. These fungi can help the tree’s root system spread faster and optimize nutrient and water absorption.

To form partnerships with symbiotic fungi, the tree’s roots must be open to aid the fungus to infiltrate and grow adequately intertwined with the tree’s roots.

This enhanced nutrient and water absorption is something of a mystery to scientists everywhere. These processes are enigmatic because there are many phenomena to describe or explain the movement of water from the roots to the crows of the trees, which are usually hundreds of feet up in the air. Some have suggested that it can be capillary action, but it’s inadequate to cover it entirely. Then there is transpiration, but that doesn’t explain the movement from bark to stem to leaf. Finally, it could be osmosis, but this is also a limited phenomenon as well. So there is forever a mystery around this action still.

One thing about trees is that they tend to grow to be very old before they die. But often, the death of a tree isn’t entirely of natural causes. As it ages, its bark begins to weaken, and thereby, the innermost layer is exposed to the agents that would only help to weaken the tree’s sturdy structure. These include fungi and opportunistic insects who will eat away the weak inner layer.

chapters.title: Survival tactics of particular trees
chapters.text:
Some trees, like the oak tree, are dominant and bursting with a desire to live and prolong their existence. When it grows in an environment that is free of competition and has ample space to bloom, the oak can be a symbol of strength and longevity, but this all changes when it grows amongst other giants as itself. Here, it cannot challenge whatever competition it has and dies off slowly, but not before making one last attempt to live. It sprouts new growth at its base, thin stems, and leaves that are meant to be a chance at a new life, but this leaves them vulnerable to existing attacks, thus leading to its inevitable demise.

Every species wants to survive, and each takes from the others what it needs. All are basically ruthless, and the only reason everything doesn't collapse is because there are safeguards against those who demand more than their due. ~ Peter Wohlleben

But not all trees are as selectively strong as the oak; others can grow and thrive in whatever soil or ecosystem they end up in. This adaptability is an essential trait for the species that are needed the most in nature because if they were only selectively adaptive, the organisms and events that require the existence of these trees would suffer significantly as a result. So what qualifies a tree as a tree? Some argue it’s the height above ground or the branch configuration or maybe the thickness of its stem, but those factors are inadequate to classify a plant as a tree amply.

The proper parameter for tree classification is the presence of roots, the more prominent the root network, the more chance it stands to be termed a tree.

Did you know? According to Nitrofill, by planting 20 million trees, the earth and its people will be provided with 260 million more tons of oxygen. In addition, those same 20 million trees will remove 10 million tons of CO2.

chapters.title: Trees and their nurture of the ecosystem
chapters.text:
Trees contribute significantly to the ecosystem’s health, supply oxygen, provide much-needed water for rejuvenation, and allow the resident plant and animal life to heal.

There are many concerns about climate control and how trees are vital for a healthier climate system. We know that trees and plants take in carbon dioxide and give off oxygen. This priceless exchange feeds a vast array of forest creatures and the human population at large.

Trees also play a role in water economy and toxicity control. During the rainy season, the trees take up a lot of water, effectively acting like a sponge and store it for the times when there’ll be less rainfall. This phenomenon is a very vital part of nature as regards the health of the ecosystem. The stored-up water is fed back into the ecosystem once the winter passes and the forest life needs replenishments.

Trees form a carbon dioxide vacuum, which means a network of trees can store large amounts of the harmful greenhouse gas whilе efficiently emitting much-needed oxygen.

But how does the water stored up by the trees get back to the land? When the rains fall and drench the forest floor, and the trees take in as much water as possible, it is kept, waiting for the springtime. Then, when the harsh cold of winter passes, gravity aids the flow of water by pulling the water from every point to the lowest point, a form of draining action, thus removing the adequate amount of water needed for the ecosystem to function.

For a truly healthy ecosystem to exist, there must be some form of harmony and interdependent system that benefits both parties involved, but this isn’t so; the reality is that there is a pest and a host for every relationship. One organism benefits from the relationship while one is getting fed off.

Nature isn’t a fairytale story, and this is where the trees play a vital role in supporting the resident animal and plant life — even the pests.

chapters.title: A Tree’s life is rife with exposure to mortal dangers but can adapt
chapters.text:
If a tree is left undisturbed, it can live for a very long time. But the change in weather and the environmental conditions cause its bark to shrink and expand. Ordinarily, if a tree is infested by a foreign fungus or insect, it simply shores up its bark. It covers up whatever wound was previously inflicted by the infestation, but when they are plagued by weather inconsistencies, the injuries simply worsen and worsen.

Light plays a very crucial role in tree health and survival as well. Hence, the location of a planted tree determines how healthy the tree will turn out to be. A lot is dependent on the location, availability of similar species, and overall environment. A tree will do better when it is surrounded by trees similar to it. But most importantly, a tree will flourish if it's planted in its original habitat. It has an adequate nutrient supply, sunlight, and a healthier network of roots between its neighboring trees.

Sunlight doesn’t just foster tree growth and health; it encourages the growth of other organisms that significantly affect the tree’s overall health.

But there are trees — rogue species — that thrive better in new and foreign places. These trees are designed to adapt quickly and aggressively to the new environment. They are known as pioneer species. They are characterized by thicker barks and deeper roots. Their adaptive style is so that they settle in quicker to their new environment.

For a tree to be propagated, either to a similar terrain or a foreign one, it needs the help of nature. Trees can’t move, not like other creatures, and seed propagation has to be a creative effort. For example, some trees have light, feathery seed designs that help them float on the flow of the wind; some have slightly heavier ones, but with a specialized winged design that aids wind propagation. In contrast, some trees with much heavier seeds rely on forest creatures that would carry and store the seeds somewhere later and forget them there.

chapters.title: Trees can live for extended periods
chapters.text:
The life expectancy of trees ranges from a few hundred years to tens of thousands of years, but how do they live that long? Studies have shown that regardless of the weather conditions or change in climate and its environment, a tree would find a way to continue to exist, maybe not in total but in a part that wholly represents the tree’s identity and life.

A host of natural disasters plague the longevity and survival of the trees and their forest community. For example, a perfectly healthy forest can suffer great tragedy in the event of a wildfire; many trees will perish in the disaster, and if left unchecked, the whole forest too. A tornado could also wreak considerable damage to forest life and threaten the structure and stability of the ecosystem.

During times of turbulence and turmoil, trees find a way to continue even under the ash of a wildfire.

Speaking of forest life, members of the tree community initially occurred in a particular region, and some were transported there. The immigrants pose a threat or challenge to the native species in that it can grow much faster and is free of the susceptibility to the external elements that affect their growth and health. They often outshine the natives and thrive in the new environment, sometimes owing to the harsh conditions they were migrated from.

As the health or favorability of an environment is concerned, the trees resident there also play a role. Certain tree species can reduce germs, filtering the air to a clean and healthy state. Others can influence the quality of oxygen available for animal life to benefit from also. The more the number of these individual species in a locale, the healthier the atmosphere and the better chance the trees and forest life stand to thrive.

Due to the immense contribution the trees have played throughout our human existence and their vital role in the ecosystem, specific legislation has been set up to protect them from rampant harvesting and exploitation. However, the trees do a lot and would be wise if left undisturbed to work more efficiently.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Years and years of misconception and maltreatment of the trees can be redeemed today; we need to learn more about their importance and why they need to be preserved. We need in-depth knowledge of their society, health, language, and mode of transport. To truly appreciate nature, we must dive deeper into it and see for ourselves, trees do so much for life on earth and don’t get enough credit; instead, they are cut down by just about everything from storms to fires to man’s desire for the industry. We need to stop this atrocity today; trees have feelings too.

Just like the animals the trees feed, shelter, and support, trees experience a kind of hibernation too. They shed their old leaves and form the groundwork for a new set of leaves and a fresh new toughened bark.

Trees might indeed have a sense of time and operate with regards to said time, often aligning to the time of nature; this usually signals the seasons and affects a change in the look of the tree itself. They sense the change in the length of days and the temperature fluctuation and adjust themselves to a possibly harsh winter.

Trees could solve the problems if people trying to improve things would only allow them to takeover. ~ Peter Wohlleben

This summary also discussed how trees provide oxygen, water, and valuable shade and provide a home for the animals in the forest. Particular creatures have made their homes in the treetops and even in the sturdy branches. Fell trees, or those that fell of their own accord, also provide needed shelter and safety for the many animal populations in the forest.

Many creatures have made their homes in the trees throughout their evolution. There is a place for insects, rodents, birds, and the rest of the curious populace. Trees also help other trees; the dead ones act as feed for the newly born trees. The remnants of the dead tree{s} offer shelter and a source of nutrients for the growing trees.

Try This

With all the highlighted benefits of trees to the planet, it becomes plain that deforestation is self-sabotage.
• Plant a tree today; they are life-givers.
• Cities should incorporate green belts in their master plan, just like we have in New York’s Central Park.
• Cut down on summer energy expenses by allowing trees and greenery to thrive on your residential property.
